content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814


May 19, 1976                                                        A - 76-05-034


Mr. Roy S. Kropke
City Clerk, City of Monrovia
415 South Ivy Avenue
Monrovia, California  91016

Dear Mr. Kropke:

Thank you for your letter of May 14, requesting clarification of
the reporting requirements for city council candidates.

A candidate who chooses to receive and expend all money in con-
nection with his/her candidacy may do so without registering
with the Secretary of State as a "recipient committee" under the
Political Reform Act.  The committee definition is used to
determine who has reporting requirements under the law.  Since
the candidate already must file in connection with his/her
campaign, there is no purpose served by requiring the candidate
to also become a committee.

The candidate is required to report all money received and spent
in connection with his/her candidacy whether or not the money is
contributed by others or personal funds are used.  If $200 or
more is spent on behalf of the candidacy, a long form, Form 470,
must be filed.

I trust this answers your question.  If not, please do not hesitate
to let me know.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        REGINA SICILIANO-KUTCHINS
                                        Technical Assistance Program
                                        Coordinator

RSK:mak
  bcc:  Jeanne - A




TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE/ADMINISTRATION (916) 322-5660  •  EXECUTIVE/LEGAL  322-5901  •  ENFORCEMENT/CONFLICT OF INTEREST  322-6441

City of Monrovia
Incorporated 1887

415 SOUTH IVY AVENUE - MONROVIA, CALIFORNIA
Phone 359-3231                                91016


May 14, 1976


Fair Political Practices Commission
Technical Assistance and Analysis Division
1100 "K" Street
Sacramento, Ca. 95814

Dear Sir:

     In a small city municipal election, it happens
that candidates running for a city council office
do not have committees to collect money and make
payments for advertising expense. The candidate
himself accepts contributions from various people
who want to contribute toward his campaign. Suppose
he collects $400 from the citizens and has a post
card mailing or brochure mailing that costs him
$650 for printing and postage. He, the candidate,
paid the bill of $650 by writing his own check for
$250. He does all this himself. Now, does he
have to file a Committee Statement and a Candidates
Statement, plus applying for a Committee identifi-
cation number?

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Roy S. Kropke
                                        City Clerk


RSK:mm

sections.question: In a small city municipal election, it happens that candidates running for a city council office do not have committees to collect money and make payments for advertising expense. The candidate himself accepts contributions from various people who want to contribute toward his campaign. Suppose he collects $400 from the citizens and has a post card mailing or brochure mailing that costs him $650 for printing and postage. He, the candidate, paid the bill of $650 by writing his own check for $250. He does all this himself. Now, does he have to file a Committee Statement and a Candidates Statement, plus applying for a Committee identification number?
sections.conclusion: A candidate who chooses to receive and expend all money in connection with his/her candidacy may do so without registering with the Secretary of State as a 'recipient committee' under the Political Reform Act. The candidate is required to report all money received and spent in connection with his/her candidacy whether or not the money is contributed by others or personal funds are used. If $200 or more is spent on behalf of the candidacy, a long form, Form 470, must be filed.
sections.question_synthetic: Must a city council candidate who personally collects contributions and pays campaign expenses file committee statements and obtain a committee identification number, or can they operate as an individual candidate?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Candidates may receive and expend campaign funds without registering as a committee, avoiding the need for a committee identification number. However, they must still file candidate reporting forms (Form 470 if $200+ is spent) disclosing all money received and spent on their candidacy.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both the incoming request letter (May 14) and the FPPC response letter (May 19). Formal Q/A sections not explicitly labeled but clearly identifiable. High confidence in extraction; minor formatting issues in original document do not affect clarity of the substantive content.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: In a small city municipal election, it happens that candidates running for a city council office do not have committees to collect money and make payments for advertising expense. The candidate himself accepts contributions from various people who want to contribute toward his campaign. Suppose he collects $400 from the citizens and has a post card mailing or brochure mailing that costs him $650 for printing and postage. He, the candidate, paid the bill of $650 by writing his own check for $250. He does all this himself. Now, does he have to file a Committee Statement and a Candidates Statement, plus applying for a Committee identification number?

CONCLUSION: A candidate who chooses to receive and expend all money in connection with his/her candidacy may do so without registering with the Secretary of State as a 'recipient committee' under the Political Reform Act. The candidate is required to report all money received and spent in connection with his/her candidacy whether or not the money is contributed by others or personal funds are used. If $200 or more is spent on behalf of the candidacy, a long form, Form 470, must be filed.

The candidate himself accepts contributions from various people who want to contribute toward his campaign. Suppose he collects $400 from the citizens and has a post card mailing or brochure mailing that costs him $650 for printing and postage. He, the candidate, paid the bill of $650 by writing his own check for $250. He does all this himself. Now, does he have to file a Committee Statement and a Candidates Statement, plus applying for a Committee identifi- cation number? Sincerely, [signature] Roy S. Kropke City Clerk RSK:mm
embedding.summary: The FPPC clarifies that city council candidates can operate as individual candidates without forming a committee or registering with the Secretary of State, but must file appropriate candidate disclosure forms if campaign expenditures reach $200 or more.
